ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Che Ba Mau specifically. Closest parent would be a generic sweet bean dessert or coconut milk dessert category. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sweet dessert drink with coconut milk — closest parent match for Vietnamese layered bean and jelly dessert drink
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GBQ is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a coconut-milk-based sweet dessert drink. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Che Ba Mau.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Che Ba Mau or a sufficiently close parent concept in the food hierarchy.

preparation_method: served with coconut milk and crushed ice

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one tall glass of approximately 350g, containing layered sweetened red kidney beans, mung bean paste, pandan agar jelly strips, coconut milk, and crushed ice.

nutrient_source.title: Che Ba Mau — component calculation from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table 2020 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (mung beans, red kidney beans, coconut milk, agar, sugar)
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Che Ba Mau as a composite dish. Values are derived by component calculation: sweetened red kidney beans (USDA SR Legacy #16033 + sugar addition), mung bean paste (USDA SR Legacy #16080 + sugar), pandan agar jelly (agar USDA SR Legacy #11665 + pandan extract negligible), coconut milk (USDA SR Legacy #12117), sugar syrup, and crushed ice. Proportions estimated from standard Southern Vietnamese recipe: ~80g red beans, ~70g mung bean paste, ~60g pandan jelly, ~80g coconut milk, ~60g crushed ice per 350g glass. FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 used for regional cross-validation of bean and coconut milk values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened bean and coconut milk desserts — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened red beans (GI ~43), mung bean (GI ~31), and coconut milk fat moderation effect; composite GI estimated at ~60 for the full sweetened layered preparation
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Che Ba Mau as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~60 is estimated from: sweetened red kidney beans (GI ~43, University of Sydney GI Database), sweetened mung bean paste (GI ~31 for mung bean, elevated by added sugar syrup to ~55–65 range), pandan agar jelly (negligible carbohydrate contribution to GI), coconut milk fat (~2.3g/100g, ~8g per serving) moderating absorption. Weighted composite GI estimated at ~60 (medium). GL per 350g serving = (60 × 42g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 25.2. This is consistent with comparable Southeast Asian coconut milk bean desserts in the literature.

hypertension.guidance: Che Ba Mau is low in sodium (~17mg/100g, ~60mg per glass) and poses no acute hypertension concern from sodium load; however, the saturated fat content (~8g per serving from coconut milk) warrants moderation for patients managing cardiovascular risk alongside hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar load (~38g per serving) and medium GI (~60) producing GL ~25.2 per glass
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid Che Ba Mau due to approximately 38g of sugar and a glycaemic load of ~25.2 per standard glass; if consumed, requesting reduced sugar syrup on the bean components can save approximately 10g of sugar and meaningfully lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8g per serving from coconut milk (lauric acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk in Che Ba Mau contributes approximately 8g of saturated fat per serving (predominantly lauric acid), which may raise LDL cholesterol with regular consumption; patients with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional dessert and not a daily food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chè Ba Màu (Che Ba Mau) is a Vietnamese three-colour layered dessert drink originating from Southern Vietnam (Saigon / Ho Chi Minh City). The name means 'three-colour sweet soup' in Vietnamese. It is served in a tall glass with three visually distinct layers: sweetened red kidney beans at the bottom (red), pandan-flavoured agar jelly strips in the middle (bright green), and yellow mung bean paste on top, all finished with sweetened coconut milk poured over crushed ice. The dessert is refreshing, mildly sweet, and creamy from the coconut milk, with a soft-chewy texture from the jelly and beans. Per standard glass (~350g): approximately 259 kcal, 4.9g protein, 8.1g fat (~7g saturated from coconut milk), 42g carbohydrate (~38g sugar), 3.5g dietary fibre, ~60mg sodium. GI ~60 (medium), GL ~25.2 — high glycaemic load driven by added sugar syrup on the bean components. The coconut milk fat moderates GI slightly but does not prevent a significant postprandial glucose rise. Very low sodium. Vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Available at Vietnamese restaurants and dessert shops in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Clinically relevant: high sugar load makes this unsuitable for regular consumption by patients with Type 2 Diabetes; saturated fat from coconut milk is a cardiovascular consideration with frequent intake. Source: component calculation from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence: medium.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Che Ba Mau as a composite dish. Values are derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 using standard Southern Vietnamese recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for the component foods. Medium confidence is appropriate for a well-characterised traditional dish estimated from approved component sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-calculated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (mung beans #16080, red kidney beans #16033, coconut milk #12117, agar #11665) and FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020, using estimated proportions for a standard 350g Southern Vietnamese serving. 2. water_g set to -1: water content of this composite dish (which includes a variable crushed ice component) cannot be reliably calculated from component sources without direct moisture analysis of the assembled preparation; neither USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy nor FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 reports a water_g value for this composite dish or a sufficiently close analogue. This is documented as data not available per G3 numeric hygiene rules. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): all ingredients are plant-based; cholesterol is naturally zero. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats are used. 4. GI ~60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Che Ba Mau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.05) are estimated from coconut milk fatty acid profile (USDA SR Legacy #12117); these are minor contributors. 6. The input record cited FAO/INFOODS as a source; this is an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy for Southeast Asian dishes. USDA SR Legacy is used as the secondary component anchor.
